title: Apply: The Reconstruction Amendments
description: Apply your knowledge of the three Reconstruction Amendments to this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator John Henderson’s statements during an 1865 congressional debate.**

Then we all admit that slavery is wrong, and that its friends have driven the country to unnecessary war. If they made war once, they may make it again. We cannot remove anti-slavery [[☃ definition 1]]. Therefore the restoration of slavery is a restoration of political strife. History will repeat itself. New wars will come. The innocent will suffer again. . . .

If the slavery question shall be settled by constitutional amendment, . . . the question will be settled beyond all judicial [[☃ definition 2]]. The public mind will rest in the conviction that slavery is abolished. To abolish it in any other way must be attended with doubts of its legality.

**Which statement best summarizes the main idea of the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Civil War settled the slavery issue, so there is no need for a constitutional amendment.
The federal government has no power to settle the slavery issue.
The judicial branch is encouraging conflict between the North and South over slavery.
There needs to be a formal change to the Constitution so that it bans slavery.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator John Henderson’s statements during an 1865 congressional debate.**

Then we all admit that slavery is wrong, and that its friends have driven the country to unnecessary war. If they made war once, they may make it again. We cannot remove anti-slavery [[☃ definition 1]]. Therefore the restoration of slavery is a restoration of political strife. History will repeat itself. New wars will come. The innocent will suffer again. . . .

If the slavery question shall be settled by constitutional amendment, . . . the question will be settled beyond all judicial [[☃ definition 2]]. The public mind will rest in the conviction that slavery is abolished. To abolish it in any other way must be attended with doubts of its legality.

**Which amendment to the Constitution achieved the result that Henderson calls for in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Twelfth Amendment
The Thirteenth Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from John Bingham’s comments in an 1865 session of the 39th Congress.**

The spirit, the intent, the purpose of our Constitution is to secure equal and exact justice to all men. This has not been done. It has failed to be done in the past. It has failed in respect of white men as well as black men. . . .

To be sure, it was not because the Constitution of the United States [[☃ definition 1]] any infringement of [Black men’s] rights in that behalf, but because in defiance of the Constitution its very guarantees were disregarded. I call the attention of the House to this fact merely for the purpose of suggesting for their consideration, that in view of all that has happened; in view of the fact that many of the States—I might say, in some sense all the states of the Union—have [[☃ definition 2]] violated the absolute guarantees of the Constitution of the United States to all its citizens, it is time that we take security of the future, so that like occurrences may not again rise to distract our people and finally to dismember the Republic.

**Which statement best summarizes the main idea of this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: States have not treated people fairly, despite the fact that the Constitution promises fairness.
States do not have the power to prevent race-based discrimination.
States must follow the laws of the federal government, as the Constitution says.
States that have rebelled against the federal government must be held accountable and punished.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from John Bingham’s comments in an 1865 session of the 39th Congress.**

The spirit, the intent, the purpose of our Constitution is to secure equal and exact justice to all men. This has not been done. It has failed to be done in the past. It has failed in respect of white men as well as black men. . . .

To be sure, it was not because the Constitution of the United States [[☃ definition 1]] any infringement of [Black men’s] rights in that behalf, but because in defiance of the Constitution its very guarantees were disregarded. I call the attention of the House to this fact merely for the purpose of suggesting for their consideration, that in view of all that has happened; in view of the fact that many of the States—I might say, in some sense all the states of the Union—have [[☃ definition 2]] violated the absolute guarantees of the Constitution of the United States to all its citizens, it is time that we take security of the future, so that like occurrences may not again rise to distract our people and finally to dismember the Republic.

**Which of the following eventually secured the protections that Bingham describes in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The requirement that former Confederate states guarantee universal voting rights for men
The emancipation of all enslaved people across the United States and prohibition of the institution of slavery
The ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution
The elimination of barriers to voting for Black men and women, like literacy tests and poll taxes

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from President Andrew Johnson’s veto of the Civil Rights Bill of 1866.**

If, as is claimed by many, all persons who are native born already are, by virtue of the Constitution, citizens of the United States, the passage of the pending bill can not be necessary to make them such. If, on the other hand, such persons are not citizens, as may be assumed from the proposed legislation to make them such, the [[☃ definition 1]] question presents itself whether, when eleven of the thirty-six States are unrepresented in Congress at the present time, it is sound policy to make our entire colored population and all other excepted classes citizens of the United States. Four millions of them have just emerged from slavery into freedom. Can it be reasonably supposed that they possess the [[☃ definition 2]] qualifications to entitle them to all the privileges and immunities of citizens of the United States? Have the people of the several States expressed such a [[☃ definition 3]]? It must also be asked whether it is necessary that they should be declared citizens in order that they may be secured in the enjoyment of the civil rights proposed to be [[☃ definition 4]] by this bill.

**How does Johnson attempt to justify his veto of the Civil Rights Bill?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He argues that people who were only recently freed from slavery are not qualified to be citizens.
He argues that the bill does not provide enough protections for formerly enslaved citizens.
He suggests that the bill is not valid because the southern states are not currently represented in Congress.
He suggests that African Americans have expressed strong opposition to the bill.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Joseph Noxon’s 1865 letter to President Andrew Johnson.**

You say you believe in democratic government, or *consent*  of loyal people. Yet you *dare not* [[☃ definition 1]] with practical effect the right of the colored man to vote. Are you honest?

You [[☃ definition 2]] to protect loyal men & to punish traitors; yet you *refuse* the [[☃ definition 3]] to loyal colored people, the *only* means effectual for their protection and advancement.

You know rebels disappointed will wreak revenge on loyal blacks, & yet you refuse to the franchise for their protection. 

You say you have no right to grant it. You know in the first elections to be held to reorganize a seceded state *you* have the *power,* the *right,* & the *duty* to say who shall vote. Otherwise rebels will re-elect rebels, as witness Virginia.

**Which statement best summarizes Noxon’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Without complete and total emancipation, slavery will continue across the United States.
Without an amendment forcing states to comply with federal legislation, southern states will just secede from the Union again.
Without any military personnel to enforce order, Black people will face violence from those loyal to the Confederacy.
Without the right to vote, Black men will be vulnerable to discrimination and violence in southern states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Joseph Noxon’s 1865 letter to President Andrew Johnson.**

You say you believe in democratic government, or *consent*  of loyal people. Yet you *dare not* [[☃ definition 1]] with practical effect the right of the colored man to vote. Are you honest?

You [[☃ definition 2]] to protect loyal men & to punish traitors; yet you *refuse* the [[☃ definition 3]] to loyal colored people, the *only* means effectual for their protection and advancement.

You know rebels disappointed will wreak revenge on loyal blacks, & yet you refuse to the franchise for their protection. 

You say you have no right to grant it. You know in the first elections to be held to reorganize a seceded state *you* have the *power,* the *right,* & the *duty* to say who shall vote. Otherwise rebels will re-elect rebels, as witness Virginia.

**Which event would eventually address the concerns that Noxon raises?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The establishment of the Freedmen’s Bureau
The implementation of General Sherman’s Field Order 15
The ratification of the Fifteenth Amendment
The passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1875

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Henry Wilson’s speech in a Senate debate on April 8th, 1865.**

If this amendment shall be [[☃ definition 1]] by the will of the nation into the Constitution of the United States, it will [[☃ definition 2]] the last lingering vestiges [dw: remains] of the slave system . . . from the face of the nation it has scarred with moral [[☃ definition 3]], from the bosom of the country it has reddened with the blood and strewn with the graves of patriotism. The incorporation of this amendment into the organic law of the nation will make impossible for evermore the re-appearing of the discarded slave system, and the returning of the [[☃ definition 4]] of the slave-master’s domination.

**Based on the excerpt, which amendment does Senator Wilson support?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: cruel use of power and authority

choices.content: The Twelfth Amendment
The Thirteenth Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**This political cartoon most closely relates to which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The political cartoon features Abraham Lincoln opening a letter. Broken chains and a note that states “Constitutional Amendment January 31, 1865” fall out of the letter.
Broken chains was a common symbol used to symbolize freedom and liberation from slavery.
According to the title, the letter is a valentine sent by Columbia. Columbia is the national personification of the United States and liberty. She was a popular symbol in the nineteenth century.

The quote bubble over Lincoln reads, “This is like a dream I once had in Illinois.” In the years before serving as president, Lincoln emerged as a major Republican leader who opposed the spread of slavery. In his Peoria (Illinois) speech, Lincoln called slavery a “monstrous injustice.”
1865 is the year the Civil War ended, meaning the amendment was one of the first key actions taken after the war.
The correct answer is: **The ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment which abolished slavery**

options.explanation: Frank Leslie, “Uncle Abe’s Valentine Sent By Columbia,” *Frank Leslie’s Illustrated Newspaper,* [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/lprbscsm.scsm0381/), 1865.

options.alt: A political cartoon depicting President Abraham Lincoln opening an envelope. The envelope is a valentine sent by Columbia, a common symbol of the United States. Broken chains and a note that says “Constitutional Amendment January 31, 1865” fall out of the envelope. President Lincoln says “This is like a dream I once had in Illinois.
options.caption: “Uncle Abe’s Valentine Sent by Columbia” by Frank Leslie, 1865. “Columbia” is the name of a female figure who symbolically represents the United States and liberty.

choices.content: The secession of the southern states, which started the Civil War in the United States
The announcement of the Emancipation Proclamation, which freed enslaved people in the Confederacy
The ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment, which abolished slavery across the United States
The proposal of the Fourteenth Amendment, guaranteeing citizenship for Black people

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative Martin Thayer’s comments in the congressional debates about the Civil Rights Act of 1866.**

If it is [[☃ definition 1]] for the new-formed Legislatures of the rebel States to enact laws which oppress this large class of people who are dependent for protection upon the United States Government, to retain them still in a state of real servitude . . .then I demand to know of what practical value is the amendment abolishing slavery in the United States? Tell me when you boast of the glorious character of that addition to your Constitution what benefit you have conferred [[☃ definition 2]] the men who are liberated from slavery by it, if these things are to be done under the flag of the United States and the face of the authority of the Federal Constitution.

. . . I thought when I voted for the amendment to abolish slavery that I was aiding to give real freedom for the men who had so long been groaning in bondage. I did not suppose that I was offering them a mere paper guarantee. And when I voted for the second section of the amendment, I felt in my own mind certain that I had placed in the Constitution and given to Congress ability to protect and guarantee the rights which the first section gave them. 

**Which statement about the Thirteenth Amendment would Representative Thayer most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Ending slavery through the Thirteenth Amendment did not secure full freedom for formerly enslaved people.
The Thirteenth Amendment did not give Congress the power to force states to abolish slavery.
Despite the ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment, Congress could not force the southern states to rejoin the Union.
The Thirteenth Amendment was a success, as it ended slavery and forced southern states to give Black Americans their freedom.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Edmund G. Ross’s statements in the senate debate in January 1869.**

The disqualification of the free negroes of the country [from voting] before the war was one of the strong [[☃ definition 1]] of slavery, without the aid of which it could not have lived a day. It is now the last prop upon which its friends depend for its future reestablishment. Remove that and all hope for them is forever gone. 

Slavery is not dead, however, until all its supports are removed. It will never die until the negro is placed in a position of political equality from which he can successfully bid defiance to all future [[☃ definition 2]] for his enslavement. Until he is clothed with the ballot he is without that power, and is in constant danger from the [[☃ definition 3]] of men who have been and expect again to be his masters. Without the ballot he is the slave of public prejudice and public [[☃ definition 4]]—the football of public scorn . He is powerless to secure the [[☃ definition 5]] of any grievance which society may put upon him. There is not a single argument in favor of his liberation from physical servitude which does not apply with equal force in favor of his [[☃ definition 6]].


**Which statement best summarizes Senator Ross’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: additions to a larger or more important thing
a sudden change of mood or behavior

choices.content: If Congress gives Black men the right to vote, they will use that vote to punish the people who enslaved them.
States have the sole power to determine who can vote in elections, so Congress cannot force states to allow Black men to vote.
Black men were barred from voting before slavery was practiced in the United States, meaning that their lack of voting rights has nothing to do with slavery.
To achieve full freedom from slavery, Black people need the right to vote or they will be unable to fight against those who seek to take away their rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Amendment of the Constitution Regarding Suffrage,” an editorial printed in *The New York Times* in 1869.**

The [Fifteenth] amendment, furthermore, is [[☃ definition 1]] in its form,—a restriction of the power of the separate States over a matter of common interest to all the states, as composing a common nation. It prohibits any State from disfranchising any citizen,—from depriving any citizen of his right to vote,—for certain specific reasons. No citizen can be deprived by any State of his right to vote “on account of color, race, or previous condition of servitude.” These are all the restrictions put upon State action in regard to [[☃ definition 2]]. . . . Virtually, therefore, the amendment merely forbids the States from disfranchising any of their citizens, “on account of *color* or *race*.” With such other conditions as States may see fit to [[☃ definition 3]] to the right of suffrage, the amendment does not interfere.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the editorial?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment removes the power to regulate elections from the states, as states cannot be trusted not to protect the voting rights of their citizens.
The Fifteenth Amendment stops states from denying the right to vote to people because of their race, but it does not stop states from denying suffrage based on other factors.
The Fifteenth Amendment eliminates all state restrictions on voting, guaranteeing universal suffrage for all citizens.
The Fifteenth Amendment gives Black people the right to vote in federal elections, but it does not extend protections against discrimination to state elections.
